summary: O'Neil recounts a day of teaching about secrets and cryptography, featuring a guest lecture on declassified documents and her own lesson on RSA encryption. Historian Matthew Connelly discussed how digitization has made it easier both to delete archival records and to trace leaks, potentially explaining the Obama administration's harsh treatment of whistleblowers. O'Neil then taught her students about prime numbers and RSA cryptography, creating an iPython Notebook that demonstrates encoding and decoding a simple message. The post reflects on how technology shapes both information preservation and surveillance.
key_points: Digitization makes it easy to permanently delete large quantities of archival records
Digital records also make it easy to trace access and identify leakers
Obama's harsh approach to whistleblowers may be explained as 'letting the system work' - technology enabling surveillance
O'Neil created an educational iPython Notebook demonstrating RSA encryption with a complete encoding/decoding example
money_quotes: As records have become digitized, it has gotten easy for people to get rid of archival records in large quantities. Just press delete.
As records have become digitized, it has become easy to trace the access of records, and in particular the leaks.
Connelly explained that, to some extent, Obama's harsh approach to leakers and whistleblowers might be explained as simply 'letting the system work.'
Yet another way that technology informs the way we approach human interactions.
key_insight: Digitization is a double-edged sword for transparency: it makes records easier to destroy while simultaneously making those who access them easier to surveil.
